quotes.text:
I really appreciate your efforts and thoughts to using AI correctly and you are not among the ones who totally denies using AI as you said they don't know how to use it and therefore they completely ignore it. This makes them spend more and more time working on their own for the total project from start to end and give less productivity lacking behind their time and schedule and cannot emerge in the market as expected.

You have deeply hit the ball and taken out all the pro and cons of AI. It is a great assistant saving a lot of time and efforts to make you reach your ultimate goal of preparing your first draft. 

However, it depends how much you do research about the exact situation and include the real human thinkgs, feelings, experiences, stories and stand in the customer shoes to show them exactly what they want and feel happy and making them your loyal customer.

Absolutely, if it were an easy job, everyone would be a smart copywriter but AI cannot be so smart to catch the internal human feelings, thoughts, and experiences.

I totally agree with you and am doing the same with using AI, just generating ideas, outlines, doing research, analyzing the already published posts on chatgpt extracting the structure of the post and then ask chat gpt to give me the same outline using my own ideas for a specific customers having his own pain points with solutions from the real world examples.

I want to send a personalized cold DM pitch to each of these people to offer Email copywriting services for their coaching program. For each of the leads I'm pasting elow, write me a personalized script that I can use, that will include a compliment on something they've accomplished, or are accomplishing. We'll briefly explain the use case of Email marketing for their coaching business, and entice them to reply for more information. When crafting personalized scripts, utilize information hat you can find from their instagram posts, but cross reference it with other information you can find from other social media platforms like Twitter or Youtube.
Be 100% positive that the information you're gathering is of that exact same person, and you don't confuse people with the same name. Make these scripts sound friendy, but still authorative, and make them sound as human as possible.

It's less about being creative and more about showing up.

quotes.text:
@salvaged_1  I've been following him since 2022 -- where do I begin??

His work doesn't exist online except the stuff he used to write for maybe 1 hair care brand and that guy he works for (chase dimond or something). You'll notice 0% of his videos talk about copywriting skills or tips because he doesn't know anything or more than your average 19 YO copywriter. It's just lifestyle content because that's what sells courses. Big red flag.

His free courses and guides are all lead magnets for clueless writers, where he sells a $700 course and a $4-5k inner circle membership. It's ridiculously expensive and zero value compared to stuff you can find for free on copy hackers and copy that. The 1% of writers he "recruits" sometimes get jobs since there are many (not thanks to anything he teaches) and he uses that as proof to peddle his courses further. He sometimes pays them to do a success story video too. 

Some people in his circle copy Mase's business models and post their own success posts and lead magnets to a "Free course" on Insta and X that once again tricks people into buying a $700 course. When they make money from the courses, they show it as their "copywriting income" -- rinse and repeat. It's ridiculous.

quotes.text: Prompt: Write in a natural human style. Use simple, concise language with short sentences. Add occasional line breaks in between ideas, use active voice and personal examples. ask questions to engage readers. Address the reader directly as "you". Avoid overused transitions like however, furthermore, and consequently. skip formal introductions and conclusions. Don't use excessive adjectives or adverbs. Vary sentence length and structure. Include occasional imperfections or conversational elements. Never mention that you're an AI.

quotes.text:
I've been running a copywriting agency for ~5 years now and saw this play out in real time - there's always gonna be arbitrage between people who know how to recognise good copy and people who don't.

I'm living that arbitrage right now. My team switched from hiring Upwork copywriters to using Copywhiz AI for about 80% of our client work, and honestly, the AI output converts better for routine stuff. But the thing is - we still need humans with expertise to evaluate what's good and to handle the high-concept creative work.

Hormozi's point about competing against other humans, not AI, resonates hard. We're all using similar tools, but some of us wield them more effectively. The winners aren't the ones who refuse to use AI - they're the ones who leverage it while bringing human judgment and strategic thinking.
quotes.author: @UseCopywhizAI

quotes.text:
Copywriting is too much of a creative process to be replaced by AI. AI is good in math, logic in something has determined rules and boundaries, it’s good in non-divergent thinking. And you need the complete opposite for copywriting. 

TLDR - AI influence in copywriting is overblown. At best it’s going to be a tool in a copywriter’s toolbox.
quotes.author: @SuperLari1234

quotes.text: As someone at the forefront of generative AI, I've heard this argument from many that money won't matter in the future (it's not an original Hormozi thought). And I completely disagree. The nature of humans will always be to compete against other humans. As a result, there will always be a "human" element to complement AI that will provide a competitive advantage--this might be completely different concepts than we understand today, but they will evolve and exist. ... BTW, these same arguments about the need for a human workforce were made at the forefront of the manufacturing age, as well. We see how that played out.

quotes.text: Great points from Hormozi, and good questions Sean! But I don't think we'll get to that place where AI and robots 100% replace humans and humans spend their days just lying on the beach or whatever. Can't happen. A world with machine intelligence alone is fragile because it can't reproduce with zero electricity, and it can't innovate for never seen before existential dangers. quotes.text:
As a senior retired academic my vocabulary is substantial ... I have been completing my research paper, and decided to place a portion into an AI detector out of curiousity ... needless to say it was flagged as AI because the syntax was 'smooth', and the use of high level vocabulary was designated AI.
Oh dear🤦‍♂️ ... the flagged portion was then placed into a 'humanizer' ... and recalibrated ... the 'new humanized text' ... was exactly the same as what was flagged as AI. 😮
The fraudulent detection is problematic to all scholars. 
Caveat emptor ... in this instance meaning USER BEWARE.

quotes.text:
Great Info Wes! I am attempting apply what I have learned so far to my new site. I do want to add a little information about adding your tone and style report to the Custom Instructions. I noticed there are people saying that it is not working correctly.
"
I was curious about adding the tone and style report to Custom Instructions and applying it to all sessions. So I asked ChatGPT if this works. Here is what it said...

While ChatGPT Pro itself can't permanently save or recall specific tones and styles across sessions, you can definitely use Custom Instructions to guide how it should respond during a particular session. Here’s a more accurate explanation:

1. Custom Instructions: You can provide specific guidelines about tone and style in the "How would you like ChatGPT to respond?" section of Custom Instructions. This can include information from your tone and style report, but these instructions will only apply to the current session or until you update them.

2. Session-Based Guidance: For each new session, you would need to reapply or reference the tone and style guidelines from your report. While ChatGPT doesn’t retain this information across sessions, you can include the relevant details at the start of each session to maintain consistency.

So, you can use the report to inform the Custom Instructions for each session, but you’ll need to re-enter or refer to these guidelines in each new conversation.
"

I am sure it will get better as time goes on. Hopefully this is helpful info for those trying to do this.
